experiences.intentions: Key state: only 1 empty cell at bottom-left, so survival and preserving move availability are critical. Rows/cols show no immediate merges on left/right except row4 can merge 2+2 if moved right, while moving left mostly just shifts the empty space and creates no merge. Goal: maximize mobility and create an empty space without breaking the board more than necessary. Candidate actions: Up appears invalid/no-op because no tile can move or merge upward; Down is valid by dropping the 2 in column 1 into the empty bottom-left, but creates no merge and likely reduces flexibility. Left is also valid but just shifts the bottom row [0,2,4,2] to [2,4,2,0], creating no merge. Right moves bottom row to [0,2,4,2]? Actually [0,2,4,2] shifted right becomes [0,2,4,2], so no change there; but row2 [32,16,8,2], row3 [2,8,64,16], row1 [16,4,16,4] are full with no movement, making Right likely invalid/no-op. Between valid moves, Down keeps the empty cell in top-left after the column shift and may better position low tiles under higher ones; Left places empty in bottom-right but disrupts less? Since Left only moves one tile into the empty, while Down also repositions column 1 and may open future vertical structure, I choose Down as the safer non-no-op move.

experiences.intentions: Board is full, so only moves that create merges are valid progress. Current merge opportunities: row 2 has adjacent 16,16 which merge with left or right; bottom row has adjacent 2,2 which also merge with left or right. No vertical adjacent equals, so up/down likely do nothing. Goal is to keep the board movable and preferably maintain larger tiles stability while creating space. Left merges both available pairs immediately, producing [2,4,16,4], [32,8,2,0], [32,8,64,16], [4,4,2,0] before spawn, opening spaces and keeping the 64 anchored. Right also merges both, but left better preserves a smoother high-tile structure with 64 not pushed toward edge disruption. Therefore choose left.

experiences.intentions: Key state: the 4x4 board is completely full, so only moves that create merges are legal/useful. Checking rows and columns, there are no horizontal adjacent equals in any row. Vertically, only column 0 has a merge opportunity: top cell 2 and third-row cell 2 can combine if moved up or down, since the 4 between them blocks direct adjacency but compression yields [2,4,2,32] -> no merge in col0? Re-evaluating all columns: col0=[2,4,2,32], no merge; col1=[4,16,64,2], no merge; col2=[2,64,32,4], no merge; col3=[2,8,4,2], no merge. Rows also have no merges. Goal is to survive by making a legal move, but with zero empty spaces and no adjacent equal tiles after compression, there appears to be no effective merge available. Since a choice is required, pick a direction that best preserves high tiles and structure; right keeps the 64s in central-right positions and maintains monotonic tendency slightly better than left/up/down.

experiences.intentions: Board is completely full, so only moves that create at least one merge are legal/useful. Check rows/columns: left/right allow merges only on top row (2,2,4,4) producing [4,8,0,0] or [0,0,4,8]. Up/down allow a merge in first column because top and third entries are both 2 after compression: up gives column [2,4,2,32] -> [2,4,2,32] no merge actually; down similarly none. So vertical moves seem ineffective, while horizontal moves definitely merge two pairs and open spaces. Goal is to maximize space and improve structure; keeping larger tiles toward the left/top is reasonable here. Left merges top row into [4,8,0,0], opens two spaces, and keeps larger values more left-aligned than right.

Goal is to preserve mobility while creating merges; immediate opportunities are stronger with an upward move: column 0 merges 4+4 into 8, column 1 merges 8+16/64 no, column 3 shifts to create space, and crucially up keeps higher tiles concentrated near the top. Immediate merge opportunities: row3 [4,4] can merge with left/right; bottom row [0,2,2,2] can merge with left/right. Vertical merges also exist in col4 with 2 over 2 at bottom. Goal: make a safe move that creates space and avoids a full board with few moves. Candidate actions: Up appears to create no merges and would fill the only empty space, risking no empties. experiences.intentions: This is a 2048 board with several empty spaces and the highest tile 16. Key features: row 3 has two adjacent 16s at positions (2,0) and (2,1), which can merge if moved left or right; column 2 has two 8s stacked at (0,2) and (1,2), which can merge if moved up or down. Goal is to create a 32 while preserving space. Comparing moves: left immediately merges the two 16s into 32 and also merges the two 8s in the top row into 16, producing strong consolidation and likely keeping multiple empties. Right would also merge the 16s but is less corner-friendly and moves the 2 on bottom-right inward. Up merges the 8s but misses the 16+16 merge. Down also merges the 8s but disrupts structure more. Best sub-goal now is to take the guaranteed 32 and improve board compactness with left.

Goal is to preserve space while setting up merges, ideally keeping larger tiles anchored on the left. Down similarly may merge the 2s but tends to bury small tiles under larger ones and reduces control. Best sub-goal now is board organization and keeping monotonicity/space, so left is strongest.
